---
title: "Lesson 4: Who Needs Normality?"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---


::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_who-needs-normality.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_who-needs-normality.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-who-needs-normality_module5_lesson4.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## Who Needs Normality? {#sec-who-needs-normality}

Suppose

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)
\qquad
\lambda>0
$$

Construct a 95% confidence interval for the rate parameter $\lambda$.

::: {.callout-note}
## Gamma Parameterization

The gamma distribution is parameterized by shape $\alpha$ and rate $\beta$:

$$
X\sim\Gamma(\alpha,\beta)
$$

Under this parameterization,

$$
cX
\sim
\Gamma\!\left(\alpha,\frac{\beta}{c}\right)
\qquad
c>0
$$
:::

### Step One: Choose a Statistic {#sec-exponential-ci-step-one}

Use the sample mean

$$
\overline{X}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
$$

Because a sum of independent exponential random variables with common rate $\lambda$ is gamma,

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
\sim
\Gamma(n,\lambda)
$$

Therefore,

$$
\overline{X}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
\sim
\Gamma(n,n\lambda)
$$

### Step Two: Construct a Parameter-Free Pivot {#sec-exponential-ci-step-two}

Scaling by $\lambda$ gives

$$
\lambda\overline{X}
\sim
\Gamma(n,n)
$$

Critical values for this gamma distribution can be obtained directly in R:

```r
qgamma(c(0.025, 0.975), shape = n, rate = n)
```

Historically, tables were more commonly available for the chi-squared distribution. Use the identity

$$
\chi^2_{2n}
=
\Gamma\!\left(n,\frac12\right)
$$

Since

$$
\lambda\overline{X}
\sim
\Gamma(n,n)
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
2n\lambda\overline{X}
&\sim
\Gamma\!\left(n,\frac12\right) \\
&=
\chi^2_{2n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus,

$$
\boxed{
2n\lambda\overline{X}
\sim
\chi^2_{2n}
}
$$

### Step Three: Choose Critical Values {#sec-exponential-ci-step-three}

Let $\chi^2_{\gamma,\nu}$ denote the upper-tail critical value satisfying

$$
P\!\left(
\chi^2_\nu>\chi^2_{\gamma,\nu}
\right)
=
\gamma
$$

For a central 95% interval,

$$
P\!\left(
\chi^2_{0.975,2n}
<
2n\lambda\overline{X}
<
\chi^2_{0.025,2n}
\right)
=
0.95
$$

### Step Four: Solve for the Rate {#sec-exponential-ci-step-four}

Because $2n\overline{X}>0$, divide through by this quantity:

$$
\frac{\chi^2_{0.975,2n}}
{2n\overline{X}}
<
\lambda
<
\frac{\chi^2_{0.025,2n}}
{2n\overline{X}}
$$

::: {#def-ci-exponential-rate}
## Exact Confidence Interval for an Exponential Rate

A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\lambda$ is

$$
\boxed{
\left(
\frac{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,2n}}
{2n\overline{X}},
\frac{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,2n}}
{2n\overline{X}}
\right)
}
$$
:::

This interval is exact under the exponential model and does not require a normal approximation.

## Reflection
